questions.question: In the Supervisor pattern, what role does the supervisor agent play?
options: It executes all tasks directly without delegating
It monitors logs and reports errors
It stores long-term memory for all agents
It coordinates other agents, assigning tasks and combining results
questions.explanation: The supervisor acts as an orchestrator — it receives the high-level goal, delegates subtasks to specialist agents, and assembles their outputs into a final result.

questions.question: The Pipeline pattern is best described as:
options: Multiple agents competing to solve the same task
Agents passing work sequentially, each building on the previous output
A single agent that spawns copies of itself
Agents sharing a common memory pool
questions.explanation: In the Pipeline pattern, Agent A's output becomes Agent B's input, forming an assembly line where each agent specializes in one stage of the workflow.

questions.question: When would you choose a multi-agent system over a single agent?
options: When you want to reduce API costs
When the task is simple and well-defined
When tasks benefit from specialization, parallelism, or separation of concerns
When you need faster response times for simple queries
questions.explanation: Multi-agent systems shine when different parts of a task require different expertise, can run in parallel, or when keeping concerns separate improves quality and maintainability.
